1881 I (1880c). 
The sixth recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Faye. 
Spotted by Common (Ealing, near London) on 2 Aug. with 
18- and 36-inch reflectors. 
Discovered independently by Tempel (Arcetri) 
on 11 Aug. and measured first on 25 Aug. Located moving south in Pegasus; 
in October and November, located in Pisces. 
In December 1880 and January 
1881, moved north-east in Pisces; 
in February, located in Cetus; 
in the 
second half of March, 
in Taurus. 
Upon discovery, appeared as a nebulous mass with a nucleus; D=0'.5 
(10 to 11™); Pechule (Copenhagen) measured the comet on an 11-inch refrac- 
tor; Bruhns described the comet as a faint nebula 1'.5 in diameter with a 
slight condensation; nucleus 12™.5 (10 to 11™). Beginning 24 Sept., meas- 
ured in Rome with a 9-inch refractor as a 13™ star surrounded by an ir- 
regular nebula. 
28 Sept., difficult to observe due to the proximity of a 
10™ star (not fainter than 10 to 11™). 
Beginning 25 Sept., easily observed in Washington through a 26 inch 
refractor; 
a small well-shaped nucleus. 
8 Oct., Meyer (Geneva) described 
the comet as very faint through a 10-inch telescope (10 to 11™); 25 Oct., 
observed at Rome as a faint object (11™). 
30 Oct., Schmidt (Athens) meas- 
ured the comet with a 5-inch refractor, registering a 1'.5 to 1'".7 diameter 
and central condensation; nucleus of 12™.8 to 13™. 
Nov. 
1, Meyer described 
the comet as well visible; nucleus 14"(10 to 11™). 
3 Nov., Bruhns (Leipzig) 
saw the comet as an irregular nebula with small condensation; D=0!.5. 
22 Jan. 1881, Winnecke (Strasbourg) measured the comet on a 6-inch 
refractor as a bright object with a starlike central region; D=1'.5(10to11™). 
In March, appeared as a pale spot 0'.3 indiameter(12™), 
Easily observed 
by Hill (Washington) on 24 March; 
a small sharp nucleus. 
Measured last 
in Washington on 27 March and in Strasbourg on 30 March. 
Full moon on 
18 Sept., 
18 Oct. 
1880, 
14 Feb. 
1881 and 15 March. 
_

1881 II (188la). 
Discovered by L. Swift (Rochester) on 30 April near § 
Andromedae. 
Moved near§Andromedae toward the Sun. Upon discovery, 
bright comet (7™), though difficult to measure because of morning twilight. 
3 May to 11 May, measured by Borrelly (Marseilles). 
3 May, brilliant 
comet, almost circular, D=2' (6 to 7”). Observed in Vienna, Berlin, 
Dun Echt, Odessa, Hamburg, Kiel, Ogyalla and Strasbourg. 
10 May and 11 
May, Peter (Leipzig) saw the comet in twilight and in moonlight as a faint 
almost diffuse nebula (8 to 9™). Measured last on 11 May in Leipzig and 
Marseilles. 
References: A.N., 99-102; V.J.S., 
17; M.N., 41, 42; E-III. 
Orbit: Kreutz. —A.N., 
160: 186.

1881 III (1881b) (c in Baldet's catalogue). 
Large comet. 
First spotted 
below Columba by Tebbutt (Windsor) on the evening of 22 May. 
Easily 
visible to the naked eye with a 5™ nucleus (3™). 
23 May, discovered inde- 
pendently by Ellery (Melbourne); 25 May, by Gould and Davis (Cordoba). 
Moved north along the 5-65 meridians. 
At the beginning of June, located in 
Lepus; from mid-June, traversedOrion; from 22 June, observed in Europe; 
atthe endofJune, passedthroughAuriga, traversing Camelopardus; 
in July, 
passed through the polar region (23 July, 
8° from the pole); in August and 
September, observed in Ursa Minor; in October and November, 
located in 
Draco, moving almost along the 70° parallel; at the end of November and in 
December, located inCepheus; in January and February, 
in Cassiopeia. 
25 May, tail estimated at 2° in Windsor. 
3 May, Ellery spotted a bright 
starlike nucleus of 5™. 
Elkin (Cape) observed the comet from 31 May to 
4 June, estimating the brightness at 2™ and the straight tail at 5 to 6°; D=10 
to 20', nucleus 10 to 20" in diameter. 
2 June, Tebbutt saw a 9° tail; 5 June, 
measured by Gould almost in daylight. 
11 June, Gould measured the position 
of the comet near the horizon with respect to a nebulous object which later 
on disappeared; 
an assumption has been advanced that this was a case either 
of a comet split into two components or of a cometary satellite. 
Another 
assumption has been advanced that Gould confused the comet with A Eridani 
and a nearby 5™ star; Gould, however, maintained that he actually measured 
the comet. 
22 July, Krueger (Kiel) estimated the head at 1™, reporting a noticeable 
tail, 
24 July, Abetti (Padua) with the naked eye saw a 10° tail. 
23 July to 
25 July, Fabritsius (Kiev) estimated the nucleus at 2™. 
Observed in lower 
culmination with meridian instruments at Moscow, Pulkovo, Warsaw, Berlin, 
Copenhagen, and at numerous other observatories. 
Plate XXIII, Comet 1881 III, Tails after Tempel, head after Tacchini 
25 July, Borrelly (Marseilles) estimated the nucleus at 1™.5: a 20° tail 
with a relatively bright eastern edge. 
27 June, less bright nucleus; three 
plume-like jets, 
15° tail. 
29 June, brilliant jets from the nucleus to the 
Sun; 12° tail. 
Kobold (Ogyalla) measured the diameter of the head and the 
nucleus, and the position angle of the tail, 
Interesting observations of the 
head were undertaken by Schur (Strasbourg). 
Fabritsius (Kiev) estimated 
the nuclear brightness under high magnification; 24 June, 5™; 
in July 
8™. On the other hand, 
14 July, Kapteyn and Groneman (Groningen) 
258 


estimated the nucleus at 4™ with the naked eye. Intrinsic brightness of the 
comet to the naked eye was much above 5™ on 18 July. 
In June and July, Pomerantsev (Tashkent) measured the length of the 
tail and the diameter of the head. 
Schwab (Frankfurt) 
compiled the follow- 
ing brightness estimates: 
27 June, intrinsic brightness 1™, 
29 June, 2™.2; 
tail 14° long. 
In July, 3™.0 to 3™.5; 28 July, 47575} 
in August, 
6™.0 to76™.8; 
29 Aug., 
tail tracked over only 0°3 to 0°4, 
At that time, 
the comet still 
visible to the naked eye; telescopic appearance: large bright nebula elongated 
in the direction of the tail. 
In September, 
8™.2 to 8™.4; 
in October, 
8™.6 to 
97.0. 
13 Oct., Schmidt (Athens) easily observed the comet with a seeker 
(8™). 
8 Nov., abrupt drop in brightness; measurements became difficult. 
10, 
11 and 20 Dec., D=2 to 3'; gossamer-like 
nebulous 
mass impos- 
sible to measure. 
Pickering, Wendell and Searle (Harvard) photographed 
and measured the comet: 
4 Aug. nucleus 5™.9; 
30 Aug. 
77.6; 
1 Sept., 6™.1;

H,=4™.5.

(Perkins Obs. Contr.

mates in June and July, Hj) =4™.1;

and Nov., Hyj=4™.8; 
14 Feb., Hi) =3™.6. The disagreement with Bobrovnikoff 
is due to the fact that he mainly used observations referring to the nucleus 
of the comet.

1882, obtained for the 
main tail 1+ uw =1 
to 2 (type II) and for the straight faint tail observed by 
Tempel, Boss etal., 1+u=22 to 18 and g=6 km/sec (type I). 
Spectrum studied in Potsdam A.N., 
100: 301; 22 June the nucleus showed 
an intense continuous spectrum without dark lines (molecular emission); 
in 
the envelope, 
there were traces of bright bands; 
25 June, 
diffuse green and 
blue bands were observed in the bright continuous background; 
29 June, the 
three common bands were in the intensity ratio 1:5:2; the yellow band was 
the faintest. 
30 June, 
a well developed band spectrum of three bands; two 
additional bands were also reported. 
Through a high light-power instrument 
all parts of the comet and the tail over a considerable distance clearly showed 
a band spectrum. 
Polarized light emitted by the head. 
5 July, the continu- 
ous spectrum of the nucleus grew fainter; 
the bands were clearly visible. 
30 June, 
1 July and 2 July, F.A. Bredikhin (Ann. Mosc. Obs. 9(2) 1883) 
studied the spectrum; also investigated by Christie and Maunder M.N., 42 
Gassel'berg 
— Bull. Akad. Petersburg 27: 417, Konkoly —Obs. 4: 251, 
Copeland and Lohze —Copernicus, 
II. 225, Wolf and Thollon C.R., 
92, 93, 
Young-Amer. J. Sci. 22 and others. 
Huggins —Proc. Roy. Soc. 33:1 
obtained photographs of the comet's spectrum in the violet and blue regions; 
new bands detected at 3883 and 3870 A (CN) and also at 4220 to 42304; con- 
tinuous spectrum extending from the F line and beyond [lacuna in the Russian 
text here], 
N. Janssen V.J.S., 
16 and Draper obtained excellent photographs 
of the comet, showing the structure of the envelope and the tail.

1881 IV (1881c) (d in Baldet's catalogue). Discovered by Schaeberle (Ann 
Arbor) on the night of 13 July; located in Auriga, moving to Lynx. In August, 
located in Lynx; 
in the middle of August, 
in Ursa Major; at the end of 
August, 
in Coma Berenices; 
at the beginning of September, began to move 
rapidly south, passing through Virgo and Libra; 
in October, traversed 
Hydra. 
Upon discovery, brilliant object visible to the naked eye. 
19 July, excel- 
lently visible; according to Coggia (Marseilles), brighter at the center, 
which appeared to be composed of many bright points; 21 July, Tempel 
(Arcetri) described the comet as a 6™ star with a very diffuse tail 1° long. 
23 July, central condensation of 10 to 11"; 28 Aug., Coggia saw the comet 
with the naked eye. 
30 July, Abetti (Padua) reported a bright nucleus; tail 
visible to the naked eye (5 to 6™). After 31 July, Abetti described the comet 
as visible to the naked eye. 
1 Aug., Coggia easily saw the comet with the naked eye; oval diffuse 
nucleus. 
2 Aug., observations at Harvard gave m=5™.5, D= (0 
IS) ies 
nucleus visible in full moon. 
21 Aug., Tempel saw a 10° tail; 25 Aug., 
a 
brilliant 5™ nucleus surrounded by a heterogeneous dense nebula; 
a magni- 
ficent,tail:

29 Aug. pat a 
10° altitude, 
an 8™ starlike nucleus. 
15 Aug., Meyer (Geneva) estimated 
the nucleus with the naked eye at 2 to 3™. 
25 Aug., comet-seeker revealed 
a fan-shaped tail and a straight jet 10° long. 
Schwab (Frankfurt) determined 
the intrinsic brightness of the comet by comparison with neighboring stars:

12 Aug. to 30 Aug., numerous observations of the tail attempted by 
Bruhns (Simferopol'), 
Comet also observed by F.A. Bredikhin (Moscow). 
Bruhns, Noble (England) and Terby (Luvain) reported two tails: from the 
East, a curving, wide, faint secondary tail adjoining the main straight one; 
it appeared to be made up of numerous isolated branches. 
5 Sept., Peter 
(Leipzig) reported a bright comet with a 9" nucleus. 
8 Sept., Tempel could 
barely see the comet and the comparison star because of low position; 
9 Sept., bright comet; 
13 Sept., very low in the sky, visible while sinking 
below the horizon. 
10 Sept., Gould (Cordoba) photographed the comet. 
Till 
14 Sept., visible to the naked eye (6™); rapidly grew faint; 
7 Oct., too 
faint for telescopic observation. 
17 Sept., Tebbutt (Windsor) detected the comet independently with a 4- 
inch telescope as a small nebulous object in Libra; central condensation, 
no nucleus, no tail, invisible to the naked eye (7 to 8™). 17Sept. and 18 Sept. 
tail invisible; 
19 Sept., considerable changes observed in the comet; 
a 
faint tail, 
Rapidly grew faint afterwards. 
Till 15 Oct., difficult to measure 
with periphereal vision (11 to 12™), 
Measured at the Cape of Good Hope till 
18 Oct.; visible in Melbourne on 19 Oct. and 21 Oct. 
References: A.N,, 100-105; M,.N., 42, “43; V.J.S.,

the faint wide tail was

spectrum; three common bands; the green band was the brightest; the blue 
band,

1881 V (1881f) (g in Baldet's catalogue). 
Discovered in Leo by Denning 
(Bristol) on the morning of 4 Oct. 
Moved east, 
in November, 
still in Leo. 
Upon discovery, 
a small bright nebula with a central condensation (7 to 8™). 
5 Oct., Coggia (Marseilles) described the comet as suitable for measure- 
ments only for a short time after the Moon had set. 
Rapidly grew faint; 
spotted in Bonn and Strasbourg only on 18 Oct. 
Through a 5-foot refractor 
the comet appeared as a gossamer, elliptical nebula; D=11/2'; aneccentric 
condensation (9 to 10™). Winnecke (Strasbourg) reported the comet to ap- 
pear as a class J nebula through an 18-inch refractor. 
19 Oct., D=1', very 
diffuse edges, a nebulous disk at the center. 
28 Oct., faint at low altitude 
and in poor sky; appeared elongated intermittently with a 2' semimajor 
axis; easily observed with a 6-inch seeker (8 to 9™). Zona (Palermo) saw 
luminous points near the center of the nebula. 
27 Oct. , visible with difficulty. 
27 Oct. ,and 3 Nov., Schmidt (Athens) described the cometas an exceptionally 
faint nebula (9 to 10™). 
Measured also at Cambridge, 
Dun Echt, Odessa, 
Marseilles and Paris. 
Last measured in Strasbourg on 24 Nov. (11 to 12™). 
References: A.N., 100-102; M.N., 42; V.J.S., 
17; Copernicus 1-2. 
Orbit: Matthiessen. —Stw. Karlsruhe 3:179. 
Distinguished by its close 
encounter with Jupiter's orbit (up to 0.16 a.u.) and Venus! orbit (0.02 a.u.), 
with Mars! (0.06 a.u.) and withEarth's (0.04a.u.). 
Goldschmidt proposed 
identity with 1819 IV and 1855c. 
InC.A.M., Hjj=9 to 10™. 
From these 
estimates, Hjjg=9™.0+1™; 
D,=2!'. 
(vr, A): 4 Oct. —(0.85, 0.83); 
18 Oct. —(1.01, 0.90); 6 Nov. —(1.26, 1.02); 
24 Nov. —(1.41, 1.08).

1881 VI (1881e) (f in Baldet's catalogue). Discovered with a 5-inch tele- 
scope by Barnard (Nashville, U.S.A.) on the evening of 17 Sept. near J 
Virginis. 
Moved north through Bootes at the end of September and in October. 
Upon discovery and later, observed near the horizon as a circular, bright, 
highly condensed object; D=2'. 
18 Sept., Barnard reported a faint tail 
(6 to 7™), 
20 Sept. and 21 Sept., observed at Vanderbilt and Harvard 
Universities. 
On the evening of 28 Sept. Barnard saw a short tail 6 to 8! 
long. 
3 Oct., Hartwig (Strasbourg) estimated the comet's brightness at 
over 8™ through a large seeker; circular comet; D=2'; through a refractor, 
a bright nebulous mass without a nucleus (7 to 8™), 
6 Oct., described by 
Winnecke (Strasbourg) as well visible in spite of bright moonlight (full moon 
on 7 Oct.) (7 to 8™); D=11/2'; 
a very faint starlike nucleus. 
Measured on 
261 


7 Oct. (full moon). 
8 Oct., Schmidt (Athens) saw the comet with a 5.5-inch 
refractor but could not measure it (8 to 9™). 
11 Oct., Peter (Leipzig) des- 
cribed the comet as a gentle nebulous mass; 
in spite of cirrus, appeared 
as a bright nebula with central condensation (8 to 10™). 
18 Oct., Rumker 
(Hamburg) saw the comet as a fair-sized, 
very pale object. 
16 Oct. and 
18 Oct., Millosewich (Rome) measured the comet with difficulty on a filar 
micrometer (10 to 11™). 
21 Oct., measurements impossible. 
Observed 
also at Kiev, Nikolaev, 
Paris, Vienna, Berlin and Geneva; measured last 
in Washington on 27 Oct. 
References: A.N., 
100-103; V.J.5., 
17; M.N., 
42; E-III. 
Orbit: 
Millosewich. —A.N., 
102: 269. 
Absolute magnitude Hig after E-III]. 
D,=2.0 to 21.2: 18 Sept. to 28 Sept. 
S=0.002. 
(r, A): 17 Sept. —(0.47, 1.11); 6 Oct. —(0.71, 1.44); 23 Oct. ms i A 
1.60).

1881 VII (1881d) (e in Baldet). 
The twenty-third recorded apparition 
of the Comet Encke-Backlund. 
Spotted according to Backlund's ephemeris 
on 20 Aug. in Strasbourg and Leipzig (12™), on 21 Aug. in Arcetri and on 
24 Aug. in Pulkovo. 
Located in Perseus; 
rapidly moved east; 
in September- 
October passed Auriga, Lynx and Leo; 
in November, located in Virgo. 
20 Aug., too faint to bear illumination; 
24 Aug., hardly visible through 
a 15-inch refractor (13™). 
25 Aug., observers in Leipzig described it as 
a nebulous gossamer spot. D=4' (10 to 12™). In September, 
an increase in 
brightness, though the comet still remained pale and without a nucleus 
for some time. 
19, 20 and 22 Sept., Schmidt (Athens) easily saw the comet 
through a seeker (9 to 10™). 
At the end of September, 
a nucleus observed; 
through a seeker, brighter than the 9™ comparison stars (8 to 9™). 
19 Oct., 
Schmidt saw the comet through a seeker in moonlight as a large brilliant 
object with substantial condensation; visible to the naked eye (6 to 7™). 
Same shape as reported by Schmidt in October 1848. 
21 Oct., Barnard 
saw the comet as a very bright circular object with moderate condensation; 
well visible through a 30-mm seeker (7 to 8™). 
26 Oct., 
in Pulkovo, 
at 6:00 A.M. 
in the morning twilight, 
the comet 
was still bright, disappearing only at 6:15 A.M. 
27 Oct., with the naked 
eye Schmidt saw the comet without certainty; through a refractor, 
dense, 
white object without a nucleus. 
A narrow 5 to 7' tail. 
28 Oct., brightness 
estimated at Pulkovo at 6 to 7™ 
4 Nov., Schmidt saw the comet last before 
the perihelion passage through a refractor when low above the horizon; 
very small white, brilliant, dense nebula with a sharp-point tail 7' long. 
Through a seeker appeared as a diffuse star of 5". 
Schmidt reported varia- 
tion in comet's diameter at approach to the Sun (A.N., 101:297). 
6 Nov., 
Winnecke (Strasbourg) estimated the brightness at 6 to 7™ through a seeker 
{x30 magnification); 
through a telescope, 
a circular central disk 50'' in 
diameter. 
5 Nov., Tacchini (Rome) saw the comet with the naked eye; ob- 
servations difficult due to bright sky and low altitude. 
Observed in Moscow 
till 
7 Nov., 
in Rome till 11 Nov. 
Bright object at the visibility threshold 
of the naked eye. 
References: A.N., 100-102; Ann. Mosc.. Obs. 2(9): 114; V.J.S., 
17; A.N., 
119:63; Holetschek IV, p. 78. 
Orbit: Backlund. —Mém,. Acad. Petersburg 34(8).

1881 VIII (1881h). 
Discovered by L. Swift (Rochester, U.S.A.) on 16 Nov. 
in Cassiopeia at 15° from the pole. 
Moved south through Cassiopeia, 
Andromeda (December) and Pegasus (December 1881, January 1882). Upon 
discovery, 
faint, slightly condensed nebula (8 to 9™). 
Due to a telegraphic 
error, observed in Europe only at the end of November. 
25 Nov. to 26 Nov., 
Winnecke (Strasbourg) easily observed the comet with 41/2-inch seeker; 
faint nebulous mass, 
D=4!' (8™); through an 18-inch refractor, 
D=1!'. 
10 Dec. to 21 Dec., Schmidt (Athens) saw the comet as a very pale object 
through a 51/2-inch refractor (8 to 9™); D=2.6 to 4!.2. 
According to Palisa 
(Vienna), comet was faint in December (9 to 10™). 
18 Dec., Engelhardt 
(Dresden) reported that the comet remained visible while passing near a 
7™ star (7 to 8™). 
22 Dec., Peter (Leipzig) described the comet as very 
faint; appeared elongated and with a small condensation (10™). Observed 
also at Cambridge, Dresden, Dun Echt, Konigsberg, Ogyalla, Paris and 
Rome. 
Observed last by Palisa (Vienna) on 12 Jan. 1882. 
References: A.N., 101-103; V.J.S., 
17; M.N., 42; WienStw. Ann. 2. 
Orbit: Olsson.—A.N., 
114:205.

1883 I (1883a). 
Discovered in Pegasus by Brooks (New York) on 23 Feb. 
and independently by Swift (Rochester) on the same day. Moved east; at 
the beginning of March, located in Andromeda, Pisces and Aries; 
at the 
end of March, 
in Taurus; 
at the end of April, moved to Orion. 
Initially, 
a bright comet with a faint tail and a highly condensed circular 
head, 
3 to 4' in diameter. 
26 Feb., Vogel (Potsdam) saw the comet as a 
very bright object with a 4' tail; a continuous spectrum and the three usual 
bands (6 to 7™?). 
26 Feb. 
to 3 March, Gonessiat (Lyon) described the 
comet as a bright nebula with a nucleus and a 13! tail (7™?). 
28 Feb., 
Schiaparelli estimated the nucleus at 77, D=3'. 
One tail at 
P=20° and 
second fainter one, perpendicular to the first, 
at 
P=300°; 
1 March, both 
tails visible. 
3 March, 
starlike nucleus of 7", D=3', 
tail 40' long; the 
second tail also visible. 
24 March, comet almost circular, D=2'; nucleus 
well condensed, 
but not starlike. 
1 March, Engelman (Leipzig) saw a large head and a starlike, slightly 
granular nucleus of 10™, traces of a narrow tail (P =10°); through a seeker,

1884 II (1884c). 
Discovered by Barnard (Nashville) on 16 July in Lupus 
as a diffuse nebula 2' in diameter. 
Slowly moved east almost along the 
parallel; 
in August, passed through Scorpio, 
in September, through 
Sagittarius; 
in October moved in Capricornus; 
at the end of October, assed 
to Aquarius, traversing it in November and then moving along the ecliptic. 
In the initial period, the comet was difficult to measure due to the absence 
of a nucleus or any other noticeable condensation, 
28 July, Finlay (Cape) 
reported a barely scintillating point and condensation in the nebula (9 to 10™). 
1, 5 and 7 Aug., measured in bright moonlight (9m), 
10 Aug., Tempel 
(Arcetri) reported the double structure of the condensation; very large 
nebulous mass easily visible through small telescopes and seekers (Sto. 9™). 
In August, observed in Tashkent; 
14 Aug. and 16 Aug. measured on the 
meridian circle of the Cape Observatory (8 to 9™?). 
14 Sept., Engelhardt 
(Dresden) through a 12-inch telescope saw the comet near the horizon as a 
very faint nebulous mass, D=1', no condensation, 
an intermittently scintil- 
lating tiny point. 
16 Sept., nebula elongated along the parallel, appeared 
to consist of bright and dark spots. 
15 Sept., Finlay reported sharp in- 
crease in brightness (8 to 9™). At the end of September, Perrotin (Nice) 
observed a sharp nucleus and a narrow luminous jet. The spectrum of the 
comet was also observed at Nice (not fainter than 8™). The central (green?) 
and the yellow bands were recorded; mere supposition of a third band. 
Till 24 Oct., measured by Schur (Strasbourg) and Tempel (Arcetri) (10™). 
20 Oct., Schiaparelli (Milan) saw the comet but could not measure it with 
an 8-inch telescope (10 to 11™). 
Thome (Cordoba) discontinued observations 
on 23 Oct. due to moonlight. 
5 Nov., Tempel spotted the comet easily (10 
to 11™); 6 Nov., even more clearly visible than on the previous day, but 
there were no comparison stars. 
8 Nov., an 11™ star when occulted by the 
comet, appeared as if surrounded by a haze (10™). 
15 Nov., Tempel spotted 
the comet again and tracked it till it crossed the ring (not fainter than 11™). 
16 Nov., comet not detected. 
Till 20 Nov., measured by Perrotin (Nice) with a 38-cm refractor; 
a 
whitish spot 2 to 3! in diameter on the visibility threshold. 
The absence of 
sharp condensation taken into account, 
the comet cannot be estimated at 
fainter than 11™, 
Measured also in Algiers, 
Cincinnati, Durban, Lyon, 
Washington and Windsor.

1884 III (1884e). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Wolf (1). Discovered in Cygnus by Wolf (Heidelberg) on 17 Sept. Ap- 
_ peared as a bright nebulous mass 2'.5 in diameter with a 9 to 10™ central 
condensation. 
Slowly moved south, passing in October through Pegasus 
and in November-December through Aquarius and Cetus, 
At the beginning 
272 


of February 1885, located near Mira Ceti; 
in March and April, passed 
through Eridanus. 
21 Sept., Engelhardt (Dresden), through a 5-inch seeker estimated, D=2'; 
a strong 8™ condensation 13" in diameter. 
At that time, the comet was not 
fainter than 6 to 7"; since on 22 Sept. 
it was independently discovered by 
Copeland (Dun Echt) in his spectroscopic observations of stars. 
21 Sept., 
Benko Boinik (Pola) saw traces of a tail; 24 Sept., Copeland estimated the 
diameter of the comet at 3'. 
24 Sept. to 29 Sept., many astronomers ob- 
served the comet in full moon, reporting an 8™.5 to 9™.5 nucleus (not fainter 
than 7 to 8™), 
14 Oct. to 17 Oct., observed on the meridian circle in 
Coimbra (not fainter than 7 to 8™). 
At that time, Kammermann (Geneva) 
saw the comet with a 10™ nucleus through a 2-inch seeker (7 to 8™). 
Ob- 
served in October-November at Kiel, Leipzig, Munich and other observa- 
tories as a bright object with a 9 to 10™ nucleus. 
11 Nov., Engelhardt 
(Dresden) observed an eccentric nucleus in the coma; 
intrinsic brightness 
of the comet 7™. According to Seeliger, 
a 9™ nucleus on 6 Nov. 
Till 20 Dec., observed by Franz on the Konigsberg heliometer (not fainter 
than 8 to 9™). 
In January 1885, Engelhardt and Winiler described the comet 
as fainter, though still sufficiently bright (10 to 11™). 
In March Lamp (Kiel) and Schiaparelli (Milan) with 8-inch refractors and 
Kammermann (Geneva) with a 10-inch refractor determined the position of 
the comet on a dark field (not fainter than 11 to 12™), though the comet was 
described by Lamp as a very faint, 
dull, and slightly brighter at the center. 
Observed at various European and American observatories, 
and also in 
Tashkent. 
Visible till the middle of April, but measured last on 6 April 
by Young (Princeton).

1885 II (1885e). 
Discovered in Canes Venatici by Brooks (Phelps, New 
York) on 31 Aug. Discovered independently by Common (Ealing, England) 
on 4 Sept. Moved east; located in Bootes at the beginning of September; 
in the second half of September and in October, moved through Hercules. 
Upon discovery, circular comet 2' in diameter with a 9™ central con- 
sensation, no tail (8 to 9™).

comet as bright without noticeable condensation. 
Millosewich (Rome) 
reported several bright points rather than a single nucleus. 
Observed at 
Tashkent, Hamburg, Kiev, Harrow and Berlin.

the absence of a nucleus, 
the diffuse appearance of the comet and the dif- 
ficulty of the measurements, 
6 Sept., observers in Washington measured 
the nebulous mass without nucleus or tail; external diameter estimated at 
3'.9. 
9 Sept. and 10 Sept., visible between clouds as a faint white spot 1'.2 
in diameter. 
12 Sept., Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as not a faint object, 
yet diffuse and without condensation. 
Numerous bright dots contributed 
to the granular appearance of the comet. Slightly elongated in the direction 
opposite to that of motion; D=2',

to 10™). 
15 Sept., in moonlight, D=2'.5; elongated outline. 
13 Sept.,

of irregular shape and 
without condensation. 
Measured also at Greenwich with a 6.7-inch refractor. 
16 Sept., Young (Princeton) saw the comet as a very diffuse object without 
traces of condensation; observations in moonlight very difficult. 
Measured 
last on 5 Oct. by Becker (Dun Echt) on a 15-inch refractor, when very faint 
in appearance, D=3! (11 to 12™?),

ment with different magnification will yield systematically lower results.

1886 V (1886a). 
Discovered by Brooks (Phelps) on the evening of 27 April 
above B Cassiopeiae as a quite bright circular luminous mass 1' in diameter 
with a scintillating nucleus. 
Moved through Cassiopeia; passed through 
Perseus in May, approaching conjunction with the Sun. 
After conjunction, 
at the beginning of July, located between Hydra and Monoceros, moving 
east along Hydra.

sufficiently bright (8™).

southern (foremost) part (8™).

comet, D=2', m=8™, central condensation, 
no nucleus. 
2 May to 5 May, 
observed at Ann Arbor on a meridian circle (not fainter than 7 to 8™). 
9 May, Engelhardt saw the comet as almost bright; 
a granular nucleus with 
numerous light points. 
11 May to 24 May, at lower culmination, observed on a meridian circle 
by Romberg (Pulkovo) (6 to 7™). 
18 May to 19 May, Bauschinger (Munich) 
estimated the comet as a very bright object with an 8™ clear nucleus (7 to 
6™), 
21 May to 25 May, more diffuse nucleus of 6 to 7™, appearing almost 
as a disk (5™); circular coma 2'.3 in diameter.

That the comet was seen at various 
observatories is an indication of its substantial brightness.

tion, spotted and observed from 3 July to 21 July by Tebbutt (Windsor) on

3 July,

The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Brooks (2). Discovered by Brooks (New York) on 6 July in Aquarius

D=1', with starlike nucleus and 10' tail. 
Moved

in November and 
300 


December 1889 and January 1890, moved along the ecliptic in Pisces; 
in 
February and March, located in Aries; 
in November and December 1890 
and January 1891, located in Cancer. 
In July 1889, numerous observers (Engelhardt, Holetschek, 
Spitaler, 
Barnard, Backer, Bauschinger) estimated the comet at 11 to 10™; at that 
time, the tail was estimated at 8 to 10', the head 1.5 to 3' in diameter.

1 Aug., Barnard spotted two cometary satellites, B and C, located at 
65" and 265" from the main comet, A; four other nebulous objects were 
discovered later, but the most stable were the satellites 
B and C. 
The first 
appeared as a bright object with nucleus and tail, while satellite C was 
larger in size and less well outlined. 
In the middle of August, B began to 
increase in size and grew diffuse; by the end of August, B rapidly grew faint, 
increasing in size and becoming progressively more diffuse in the direction 
towards A, as if the main comet were absorbing the satellite. 
5 Nov., B 
appeared as an exceptionally faint diffuse nebula; 
fully dissipated soon after 
(possibly absorbed by the main comet). 
Satellite C was at first fainter than 
B and 5 times fainter than A; gradually increased in brightness; 
definite 
condensation observable (nucleus). 
31 Aug., 
its nucleus and tail made the 
satellite brighter than the main comet A, which decreased in brightness for 
a few days. 
In September, A and C were equal in brightness, and A was 
3 times larger than C. Beginning the middle of September, 
satellite C 
gradually decreased in brightness, becoming considerably fainter than A 
by the end of September; 
its brightness decreased at an ever increasing 
rate, until C disappeared completely as B had; 25 Nov., C invisible through 
a 36-inch refractor. 
Satellites were observed at various observatories and 
measured with particular care by Rentz (Pulkovo) on a 15-inch refractor, 
Spitaler (Vienna) on a 27-inch refractor, and also at Strasbourg and 
Cambridge. 
24 Oct., the second satellite was bright with a 12™ nucleus and 
an elongated 21/2' coma. 
Satellites were also observed through medium- 
power instruments, 
e.g., by Kortazzi (Nikolaev), Abetti (Padua), 
Bauschinger (Munich) and others.

10 to 12™ in October, when 
considerable changes in the head were observed;

at Strasbourg, comet appeared 
bright with a 10 to 11™ nucleus and a fan-shaped jet to the north-east; 
at 
that time Plummer reported brightness variation in satellite and its approach 
to the main comet.

fainter than 10 to 11”; in December, 
11 
to 12™;

16 Dec., nebula 
2'.5 in diameter. 
20 Dec., 
at Strasbourg, 
nucleus estimated at 12™; jets 
1/2 to 1' long to the north-east.

11™; in February, 
12 to 
13™;

1890 V (1890d). 
The fifth recorded apparition of the short-period Comet 
d'Arrest. 
Discovered in Leo by Barnard (Lick Obs.) on 6 Oct., apparently, 
independently of Leveau's ephemeris. 
Very faint; moved east; at the end 
of October and in November, located in Capricornus; 
at the end of November 
and in December, 
in Aquarius. 
11 Oct., Spitaler (Vienna) described the comet through a 27-inch refrac- 
tor as a very faint object with central condensation. 
14 Oct., through haze, 
a nebula with D=2', brighter at the center. 
17 Oct., D=2'; nucleus h=13™., 
30 Oct., diffuse nebula with granular center, D=2', elongated along the 
direction of motion. 
9 Nov., D=2', from time to time appeared to extend 
over 4'; nucleus 30" in diameter, intrinsic brightness 12™. 
13 Nov., D=1 
to 
11/2'; 
14 Nov., very faint, D=1!' (13™). 
9 Oct., Kobold (Strasbourg) reported elongated pale nebula with slight 
condensation through 18-inch refractor. 
10 Oct. and 11 Oct., because of 
haze, extremely difficult to observe. 
10 Oct. to 14 Oct., Millosewich 
(Rome) described the comet as a faint indefinite object, without nucleus; 
difficult to measure (11™). 
Bauschinger (Munich), using a 101/2-inch 
refractor, saw an exceptionally faint comet; Thome (Cordoba) could not 
spot the comet with an 11-inch refractor. 
Tebbutt (Windsor), using an 
8-inch refractor, reported an exceptionally faint and diffuse comet; 
12 Oct. 
and 6 Nov., he measured the comet for some time (10 to 11™). 
Comparing 
305 


this with the 1870 apparition, 
which differed in the perihelion passage by 
only 5 days, Kreutz reported that the earlier apparition had been discovered 
considerably earlier (in July) and measured for a longer period with low- 
power telescopes. 
On the other hand, however, Barnard reported that in 
October he could see the comet easily with a 31/4-inch seeker (9 to 10™):; 
Measured last on 13 Dec. 
in Charlottesville. 
25 Feb. 1891, Barnard failed 
to detect the comet.

1890 VI (1890c). 
Discovered in Ursa Minor by Denning (Bristol) on23July 
with a 10-inch reflector. 
Appeared as a faint, circular, nebulous mass 1! 
in diameter; 
central condensation. 
Moved rapidly south; 
in August passed

at the end of August and the beginning of 
September, located in Corona Borealis; 
in the middle of September, 
in 
Serpens; 
at the end of September in Scorpio; 
in October, observed in 
Scorpio. 
24 July, Denning observed the occultation of a 9" star; the comet re- 
mained visible, and the star appeared as if surrounded by a noticeable at- 
mosphere (8 to 9™). 
7 Aug. to 9 Aug., Schorr (Hamburg) measured the 
comet with great difficulty on a 10-inch refractor with illuminated reticle 
(10 to 11™). Aguello (Palermo) also measured the comet at the visibility 
threshold of a 10-inch refractor. 
19 Aug., Engelhardt (Dresden) with a 
12-inch refractor, observed a faint, irregularly circular comet D=1', 
granular structure (10 to 11™).

noticeably granular, with a condensation; 
17 Sept., very faint (10 to 11™). 
18 Sept., faint object with jagged edges and starlike nucleus of 14™; during 
occultation of a 13.5 star, the nucleus appeared to be made up of two com- 
ponents. 
19 Sept., 
faint, 
but measured with filar micrometer (11™); 
19 Sept., Pechule (Copenhagen) with a 101/2-inch refractor reported that 
the comet appeared faint and small because of low altitude (11™). 
17 Sept., 
Luther (Hamburg) measured the comet on a 9-inch refractor, 
reporting it 
to be extremely faint. 
Last observation in the northern hemisphere on 6 Oct. 
(Lick Obs.); at southern observatories, measured for an additional month. 
1 Oct. to 7 Nov., Thome (Cordoba) described the comet as poorly visible 
on the dark field of.an 11-inch refractor; measurements effected at mini- 
mum illumination (not fainter than 11™). Tebbutt (Windsor) measured the comet 
on an 8-inch refractor with filar micrometer; 
faint and diffuse; fainter 
than Comet.d'Arrest in October.

Discovered by Barnard (Lick Obs.) on 29 March in 
Andromeda and independently by Denning (Bristol) on 30 March. 
Bright 
comet nearly 1' in diameter with a 30' tail and a 10™ nucleus (8 to 9™). 
Moved south-east, approaching conjunction with the Sun; observed last in 
the northern sky in Triangulum on 10 April in evening twilight. 
Beginning 
19 May, observed in the southern sky in Eridanus; 
at the beginning of June, 
passed to Columba; 
in the middle of June, traversed Puppis; at the end of 
June and in July, moved in Vela. 
31 March, Engelhardt (Dresden) described the comet as a bright circular 
object without nucleus D=1'; visible at first glance through a comet-seeker 
(8 to 9™). 
1 April, well visible in early twilight; D=4'; Spitaler (Vienna)

diffuse large 
nucleus of 10™; intrinsic brightness 8™. On that day, Holetschek estimated

coma also visible. 
3 April, Luther (Hamburg) described the comet as 
bright with a 9™.5 nucleus and a circular coma; 10 April, measured in twi- 
light without field illumination (7 to 6™). 
4 April, Pechule (Copenhagen) 
gave D=1'; 9 to 10™ condensation; 
faint, straight tail 6 to 8' long at 
P=12°. 
6 April, Bauschinger (Munich) observed the comet as a very bright, large 
object with diffuse nucleus (7 to 8™). 
13 April, observed by Rentz (Pulkovo) 
in twilight (6 to 7™). 
After conjunction, observed beginning 19 May by Tebbutt (Windsor); 
4 June to 13 June measured on a 41/2-inch refractor (8 to 9™); 14 June, 
exceptionally faint when observed through an 8-inch refractor (9 to 10™). 
At the end of June and on 2 July and 3 July, observed as a faint luminous 
spot (10 to 11™); 6 July, mere suggestion of a comet occasionally visible 
(11™),

Gradually grew 
faint, disappearing under the slightest illumination of reticle (11 to 12™).

1892 III (1892h). 
The first recorded apparition 
of the short-period 
Comet Holmes. 
Discovered by Holmes (London) on 6 Nov. near the 
Andromeda Nebula as a bright circular mass 5! in diameter, with a central 
condensation but no nucleus. 
8 Nov., discovered independently by Anderson 
(Edinburgh); 
9 Nov., by Davidson (Mackay, Queensland). 
Moved slowly 
south-east; 
at the end of December, near fp Andromedae; 
in January and 
February, located in Triangulum; 
in March and April, located in Perseus. 
Head grew larger: 30' indiameter atthe endof November. A faint, short tail 
was observed in the first days; Barnard's photographs showed a 30! tail. 
An isolated diffuse nebulous mass was observed 1° from the head. 
21 Nov., 
Deslandres noticed a split nucleus on a photograph; 
this phenomenon was 
not noted by him on any further occasion. 
At the end of November, visible to the naked eye as a faint, small nebula 
(5 to 6™). At the beginning of December, 
easily observed with medium- 
power telescopes. 
Rapidly grew faint; at the beginning of January, 
difficult 
to measure even on large refractors. 
Apparently the surface brightness of 
the head decreased considerably, though the intrinsic brightness as deter- 
mined by high light-power instruments did not change strongly. 
A remark- 
able flare, i.e., an increase in brightness of at least 4 to 5™, occurred 
between 14 Jan. and 16 Jan. 1893. 
16 Jan., nucleus 7 to 8™; comet visible 
to the naked eye as a faint spot (5 to 6™). Gradually grew faint; in February, 
comet appeared as in December 1892; from the beginning of March, acces- 
sible to large instruments only. Kobold (Strasbourg) saw the comet on 
6 April and on the following nights, but it was difficult to measure. 
Upon discovery, various observers (in particular Weiss, Christie and 
Denza) described the comet as very bright.

the comet as considerably brighter than M31, but smaller (4 to 5™).

1900 II (1900b). 
Discovered by Borrelly (Marseilles) on the morning of 
23 July and independently by Brooks (Geneva, U.S.A.) 5 hours later. 
Upon 
discovery, located at the visibility threshold of the naked eye (6 to 7™); 
nucleus estimated by Borrelly at 9™.5, diameter at 1 to 2'; observers 
reported a tail 1° long. Moved north; at the beginning of August, crossed 
Perseus; 
24 Aug. and 25 Aug., passed 4° from the pole; 
in September, 
crossed Ursa Minor; 
in October, moved in Draco, approaching the pole 
again; at the end of December, located 12° from the pole. 
Numerous estimates of intrinsic brightness and nuclear brightness were 
obtained by Holetschek (Vienna) with a seeker and a 6-inch refractor. 
At 
the end of July and the beginning of August, m=6™.5; the nucleus 8 to 9™; 
tail 30 to 60' long; D=3'. 
1 Aug. to 16 Aug., m=7™.2; nucleus 9™.5. 
25 Aug. to 31 Aug., m=8.0 to 8™.5. 
In the middle of September, 
m =9™.2; 
nucleus 10™, 
D=11/2'. 
In October, 
no traces of a tail, m=11™.75.

and Veenstra (Utrecht) estimated the brightness of the comet and the nucleus 
through a 3-inch seeker and binoculars. 
At the end of July, 
m=7™.0,

at the end of August,

1900 III (1900c). 
The first recorded apparition of the short-period 
Comet Giacobini-Zinner. 
Discovered in Aquarius by Giacobini (Nice) as 
an elongated nebula; brightness 10 to 11™; D=1'. 
Moved north-east; 
in 
the middle of January 1901, located in Cetus; 
in the middle of February, 
observed in Eridanus. 
24 Dec., Abetti (Arcetri) described the comet as an 11 to 12™ star sur- 
rounded by a nebula (10 to 11™); at that time, D=0'.8. Observers in Nice 
reported a small 2 to 3' tail; nucleus 11™. According to observations in 
Algiers on 26 Dec. and 27 Dec., D=1 to 2'. 
11 Jan. and 12 Jan. 1901, 
Abetti reported a 13™nucleus.

16™); 8 March, not detected with a 36-inch refractor (fainter than 16 to 17™).
